sentences.text: Leib Dimerman.
D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N.
Yes.
In time of war, when I was 14 years, I was forced to change my name.
Under Sempatia, Felix, and nationality French.
Sempatia.
S-E-M-P-A-T-I-A.
In 1926, month 10.
Yes.
I am born in Kostopol, previous Polish-- Poland.
This was a city, a small town.
K-O-S-T-O-P-O-L.
Pardon me?
It was populated-- population was 10,000 Jewish.
Rowne.
Yontel.
Y-O-N-T-E-L, Yontel.
He was born in Tichen.
T-I-C-H-E-N.
Tichen.
Furrier.
Chana.
C-H-A-N-A.
Chana.
Ptecher.
P-T-E-C-H-E-R.
Kostopol.
Self-employed.
Oh, when he was single, he was a student in [INAUDIBLE].
Now, after he married, [? it was ?] [? cut up. ?] The parents was against the marriage.
And he became a-- [INAUDIBLE] [? you ?] [? learn ?] and became professional.
Pardon me?
I have a-- a sister in Israel--
Golda.
And then I have a brother Moshe, in Israel.
He was [? enjoying ?] the army in Israel, all wars.
And one brother is killed in Israel like a hero.
His name is Meyer Dimerman.
D-I-M-E-R-M-A-N.
And beside the Kinneret, the forest, under his name, Meyer-- beside the Kinneret, in Israel, they give the name-- his name, Meyer.
He-- when the Arab attacked the kibbutz and he took care of the kids, the Arab came, and then the English army left to the Arab a tank.
And they tried to came in with this tank in kibbutz.
They would kill the kids.
Then he closed up.
He jumping in tank.
And, with his body, he closed the tank-- the-- the visibility.
And his friend put a mortar bomb, and they stopped the tank.
And a sister in Winnepeg.
My childhood?
I was attending a Polish school and a cheder.
I was attending-- I was a rabbi-- I have a rabbi what teach me-- Jewish-- Judaism.
And, like every child, we enjoy be a child.
We was playing and studying, and this was the joyment.
I started in a-- in a Polish school.
Public school.
And then I attend a cheder.
But they didn't teach Judaism.
Then I attend private cheder.
The public school was, on average, five hours a day.
In the public school, Polish.
In cheder, Yiddish.
The cheder, we attend, I think, three times a week.
After school.
Like, I'd say from 5:00 to 6:30.
And vacation time and summertime, all days.
Roughly was around 50.
Before the war, I finished grade six.
The war started.
But after grade six, start the war.
The Russian occupied.
In 1939, they occupied Poland and our city.
And this is the end of the school.
Yes.
Traditional Jewish holiday, like average family.
My mother was a very religion person.
My father a little bit the opposite.
He was liberal.
And we was watching every holiday we used to celebrate.
The real, pure, traditional Jewish way.
Shabbat, yes.
Shabbat you used to go in synagogue.
Father used to go with the kids in synagogue.
And came to synagogue, already dinner was ready.
And we used to-- yeah, I-- I came from a traditional Jewish family.
64 Targova.
T-A-R-G-O-V-A.
This is like a business street.
You call this Targova.
Yes.
You call this like 'Targova,' it's in Polish.
It'd be in English 'business district.'
Yes.
Self-employed.
In the small, little towns, and the smaller cities, we didn't feel the anti-Semitism so badly.
Now, lately, we start to feel the anti-Semitism which the Polish-- the Polish churches, they promote.
The anti-Semitism, there was preaching to the people, to the audience in church.
And this we feel lately, the students in school.
Sometime they make a cross with chalk-- a student in the back-- and we feel then the anti-Semitism.
No, no, we didn't have it, no.
Before the war, I can't answer this.
But I was 13-- in this time I was [? of ?] Poland, I was 12 year old.
I can't-- I can't tell you exactly.
I never travel.
Never travel in the big cities, before the war.
In my community is-- like I said, it was not open.
The anti-Semitism was [INAUDIBLE] not open.
[INAUDIBLE].
Anti-Semitism came out not from the government channels, only from the churches.
I like swimming, skating, skiing, ping-pong, and jumping [INAUDIBLE] in the water, fishing.
This was my main sport what I did.
Yes.
Like-- like every child, we are schoolboys.
We enjoy winter.
We were surrounded with water.
We always-- lots of lakes around.
And we liked skiing and skating with the-- with my friends.
[INAUDIBLE] it was children, like usually like kids is enjoying it.
Which the [? end ?] came-- the war, which divide us.
From the happiness-- children's happiness-- and everything was disappeared.
Yes, I have an aunt, and I have a grandfather, which I was very attached to them.
Grandmother.
And I have an uncle and some cousins.
My mother's side.
Yes.
Bar mitzvah when I was 13 year old was in Kostopol, exactly before the Russian came in to us.
I was-- I was 13 at the time of the bar mitzvah.
See, usually my bar mitzvah I don't remember, like-- I make bar mitzvah my kids.
There was friends what you know-- my friends was invited.
Maybe was around-- it's a small town.
We have around 20 people.
And we have a kiddish in synagogue.
And when we came home, my mother prepared dinner and a little kiddish wine.
And this was-- usually this is the way they make bar mitzvahs.
Oh, then, this is like a regular basis.
I was-- you attending-- when you're attending a rabbi, and you still you have to study Judaism.
In the meantime, the rabbi's preparing for the bar mitzvah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We particular didn't have it.
We have only two synagogues.
One synagogue was [INAUDIBLE].
It was a different kind district.
And what we attending in the synagogue was the big synagogue, only one.
Orthodox.
Yes.
When they came, in a short time was synagogues closed.
And people was persecuted.
Or they start [? believe. ?] They didn't give 'em opportunities.
When the people was believers in God.

And the people start to feel very [? quickly ?] and they start to acclimatize to the Russian government, and to go slowly away from their religion from believe what they believe to make a career in something, to have a better opportunity, to progress in life.
Disappeared, yeah.
Mmm-- we practiced holidays, the Russian took over, not so openly like before.
No, we practice.
Financially, it became right away extreme changes an average family [? would feel. ?] Supply food, was changes.
Yeah.
We didn't-- we was not rich people.
We was just middle-class people, which my father was not-- was not registered to the Russian like a capitalist.
He was a working man, which-- they didn't touch us, no.
Oh, very bad.
People [? were they ?] well-off, rich people, and some of their families, they send them to Siberia.
And some of them they start to investigate, in case they was bad to the communist-- the underground communist people.
And they didn't get an opportunity to get good positions.
[INAUDIBLE] treat them without trust.
Absolutely.
Absolutely.
They didn't trust capitalists.
People-- they came often a background from capitalist.
They absolutely didn't trust them.
When the Russian came in was big supply.
We have food.
No, people were scared.
Was a panic.
Everybody started to prepare himself food more like he need.
And the food disappeared from the store.
Everything was, like, overnight disappeared.
And then we was already depended on Russia have to supply.
And supply came very slowly.
Was lines-- lines for bread, lines for sugar, for this kind-- soap, some tobacco, you could buy free, without line.
Now, the other way, some, like, jam or this kind stuff to buy, herring-- and was a lineup.
And was a very bad supply.
A very poor supply.
Clothing was demand-- always a demand, Russia.
We was a very united [NON-ENGLISH].
Very united.
And my aunt was living-- was a big home-- big house.
And my grandfather was too big, the house.
And he rent it out always, one suite, for some Polish family was renting.
And my grandmother and father in the house, and I was living in the house with my parents and the kids-- my sisters and brothers.
It was a big, huge home.
Got a big garden with lots of trees-- different kind fruit.
And we enjoy, happy family.
Yeah-- Polish.
They have a business.
They rented.
Yeah.
I would say [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe 6,000 square feet?
Which here is not so big, you know? 7,000 square feet?
No, before the war, we consider this big.
Yeah.
I didn't have a separate room, no.
One room, we slept three brothers.
And the other room-- bedroom-- two sisters, and one bedroom for my parents.
A garden, yeah.
Big garden.
Oh, we used to have cucumber, tomatoes, and different kind of salad.
And the most we have trees-- apple, pears.
And we used to make our own wine, for Pesach.
From the apple, plums, we used to make our own wine.
I was in the organization, Betar.
Mhm, Betar.
Since I was young.
This is Jabotinsky.
This is extreme to the right.
Oh, we have a-- a club, [? our own. ?] And a ping-pong table.
And we have meetings.
Once in a while, we used to go out in a [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, like [NON-ENGLISH] you call this-- like [NON-ENGLISH].
We used to go out in forest.
And we used to have meetings.
And the whole meeting was based in patriotism and to stretch the hand to Israel.
Absolutely, yeah.
I-- I can't remember, exactly, you know?
Like, I am thinking roughly-- I think it was around-- maybe 250 kids?
Ages from seven, eight, and 21, 22.
We met once in a week, a Saturday evening.
After [YIDDISH].
[BACKROUND NOISE]
Yes.
We used to enjoy.
We used to-- ping-pong and the older kids used to dance and meetings.
And we used to enjoy over there, yeah.
I tell you, I don't remember exactly the street.
I remember, like, maybe like seven, eight blocks away from me, where I live, was like a-- like a house.
And big room.
One huge, big room.
And this was the location.
We have only one location.
We used to have one location.
